summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while software licenses are tedious and lawyer-filled, they are a necessary evil for any publicly released code. He shares his regret at not licensing his CodeProject articles, which ironically prevented experienced developers from using code he intended to share. Without an explicit license, code is copyrighted by default, meaning others have no legal right to use it. The post provides a comparison table of common licenses from permissive (MIT, BSD) to copyleft (GPL) to help developers choose. His core message: stop releasing unlicensed code and pick any license to enable legitimate reuse.
key_points: Unlicensed code is copyrighted by default, giving others no legal right to use it even if publicly posted
Experienced developers actively avoid unlicensed code because of legal liability concerns
The proliferation of software licenses (like standards) makes choosing one confusing, but any choice is better than none
Licenses range from maximally permissive (public domain, MIT) to restrictive copyleft (GPL) with many variations in between
The author's failure to license his own shared code created the opposite of his intended outcome
money_quotes: When I read a software license, what I see is a bunch of officious, mind-numbing lawyerly doublespeak.
A proper software license is a necessary evil for any code you plan to release to the public.
Experienced developers won't touch unlicensed code because they have no legal right to use it.
We love software licenses like we love standards – that's why there are so many of them.
Without a license, the code is copyrighted by default. People can read the code, but they have no legal right to use it.
The next time you release code into the wild, do your fellow developers a favor and pick a license – any license.
key_insight: Releasing code without a license doesn't make it free—it makes it legally untouchable, defeating the entire purpose of sharing it.
